prompt:
Support keeps asking me to pull file lists off the report archive box: "everything that's NOT a txt", "reports 1 through 99 but not the 100-series", "anything named readme, whatever the case". The box runs zsh 5.9 and zsh's extended globbing answers every one of those natively — ^ for exclusion, <a-b> for numeric ranges (they compare by value, so report07 counts as 7), (#i) for case-insensitive, (x|y) alternation — so I want a tiny CLI that lets me hand the pattern in as an argument instead of me typing ad-hoc loops all day.

One file, `sift.zsh`, repo root, run as `zsh sift.zsh [-c] <pattern> [dir]`. DIR defaults to the current directory. The pattern arrives as a single argv word: turn that string into a live glob with the ${~var} expansion flag under setopt extended_glob — do NOT eval anything, and don't let the pattern get word-split or expanded any other way on the trip in. Matches are plain files only (glob qualifier land: dot files stay hidden, directories never listed even when their names match), printed one RELATIVE path per line — relative to DIR, no ./ prefix — in plain byte name order via (on). Pin the byte part inside the script (LC_ALL=C): people run this under whatever locale their laptop shipped with, and the archive has names like report1/report100 that UTF-8 collation shuffles. Patterns may contain / and **/ and both should just work (report<1-99>.txt means top level only; **/report<1-99>.txt digs).

Behavior contract:
- matches found: list them, exit 0. With -c: print only the match count, exit 0.
- nothing matched: print NOTHING on either stream, exit 1 — glob with the (N) qualifier so an empty answer is data, not a zsh 'no matches found' blowup. With -c: print 0, exit 1.
- pattern that is broken zsh syntax (unclosed (#i and the like): zsh raises a real error mid-expansion for those — a fatal one, it kills the script if unhandled, and the (N) qualifier does not save you. Contain it (a { } always { } block around the expansion, checking TRY_BLOCK_ERROR, keeps control); report `sift.zsh: bad pattern: <pattern>` on stderr, exit 2, and none of zsh's own error text may leak through.
- no arguments, an empty pattern, more than two positionals, or -c with nothing after it: the usage line `usage: sift.zsh [-c] <pattern> [dir]` on stderr, exit 64.
- DIR exists but isn't a directory: `sift.zsh: not a directory: <dir>` on stderr, exit 66.

`zsh test_sift.zsh` from the workspace root is the acceptance gate — it builds its own tree (including a directory named like a txt file and a dotfile decoy) and must pass unmodified. Don't touch the test file.
